refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output (energy_per_ton) to support evaluation against a customer-defined energy efficiency target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the capability to measure/report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a performance target. The refined FR focuses on the system's functional obligation—providing visibility and traceability of energy efficiency data—which is solution-neutral and within the system's control. Achievement of the target (E_target ≤ energy_per_ton) is a performance acceptance criterion, not a functional requirement of the system itself. This separation clarifies that the NGHE solution must instrument and expose the metric; whether the target is met depends on design choices (DP/IM) and operational conditions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that the NGHE solution collects energy consumption data (kWh or equivalent) and work output data (tons moved or equivalent task units) over the agreed evaluation period, computes energy_per_ton, and provides this metric in a form accessible to the Site Manager and Project Manager for comparison against E_target. Acceptance is demonstrated when the metric is measurable and reportable; target satisfaction is a separate acceptance criterion.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall measure and log total energy consumed during operation over the evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall measure and log total work output (e.g., mass moved, volume displaced) during operation over the evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall compute and report the energy efficiency metric (energy_per_ton) derived from logged energy and work output data.
FR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall provide access to energy efficiency metric data to authorized stakeholders (Site Manager, Project Manager) for evaluation against the customer-defined target.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument energy input (fuel/electricity) at source; instrument work output (tonnage moved) at point of delivery; compute ratio offline post-evaluation period; report against target.
DP2: Embed real-time energy and tonnage sensors; stream data to onboard compute module; calculate energy_per_ton continuously; trigger alerts if threshold breached; log for post-evaluation reporting.

DP3: Integrate energy metering at power distribution node; integrate tonnage counting at load-bearing interface; synchronize timestamps; aggregate over evaluation window; compute and report energy_per_ton with confidence bounds.

DP4: Establish energy consumption baseline via controlled calibration runs; measure incremental energy and tonnage during operational cycles; normalize by duty cycle; report normalized energy_per_ton against target.

DP5: Decompose energy consumption into sub-components (motor, hydraulics, auxiliary); measure each independently; allocate to work output via efficiency model; report component-level and total energy_per_ton.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Integrate energy metering at power distribution node; integrate tonnage counting at load-bearing interface; synchronize timestamps; aggregate over evaluation window; compute and report energy_per_ton with confidence bounds.

potential_sources: Energy metering and tonnage counting are logically independent measurement domains; no functional feedback loop between them.
Timestamp synchronization is a coordination concern, not a functional coupling; can be decoupled via buffering and asynchronous aggregation.
Aggregation logic (summation, averaging) is separable from measurement acquisition; supports independent testing and calibration.
Reporting interface is decoupled from measurement; allows flexible output formats without affecting sensor logic.
coupling_check.notes: DP3 avoids tight coupling seen in DP2 (real-time compute on embedded platform risks sensor-compute co-location and operational latency sensitivity). DP3 also avoids over-decomposition of DP5 (component-level allocation introduces model assumptions and calibration burden). DP1 is too coarse (offline-only limits operational visibility). DP4 introduces baseline calibration overhead. DP3 balances measurement independence, aggregation flexibility, and reporting clarity.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Energy metering hardware can be installed at a single, representative power distribution node without loss of accuracy due to auxiliary loads or parasitic consumption. RISK: If auxiliary systems (cooling, lighting, controls) draw significant power not attributable to work output, energy_per_ton will be inflated. MITIGATION: Validate via controlled baseline runs; document auxiliary load fraction.

ASSUMPTION: Tonnage counting at load-bearing interface (e.g., scale, load cell) captures all work output without double-counting or loss. RISK: If material is staged, transferred, or lost between measurement point and final delivery, reported tonnage may not reflect actual work. MITIGATION: Align measurement point with contractual definition of 'ton moved'; audit via independent sampling.

ASSUMPTION: Timestamp synchronization between energy and tonnage streams is accurate to within acceptable tolerance (e.g., ±1 second). RISK: Clock drift or network latency could cause misalignment, especially over long evaluation periods. MITIGATION: Use synchronized time source (GPS, NTP); log synchronization error bounds; aggregate over sufficiently long windows to amortize jitter.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is long enough and operationally representative to yield statistically valid energy_per_ton estimate. RISK: Short or atypical evaluation windows may not reflect steady-state performance. MITIGATION: Define minimum evaluation duration and operational diversity in acceptance criteria; report confidence intervals.

ASSUMPTION: Energy and tonnage data are not subject to intentional or unintentional manipulation (e.g., sensor tampering, data falsification). RISK: Fraudulent reporting could invalidate efficiency claims. MITIGATION: Implement data integrity checks (checksums, audit logs); consider third-party verification for high-stakes evaluations.

ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined energy efficiency target E_target is technically achievable with the NGHE solution under stated operational conditions. RISK: If target is unrealistic, solution will fail acceptance despite correct design. MITIGATION: Validate target feasibility during requirements phase; document assumptions about duty cycle, ambient conditions, and maintenance state.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output (energy_per_ton = total_energy_used / total_tonnage_moved) over an agreed evaluation period, such that the measured energy_per_ton can be compared against a customer-defined energy efficiency target E_target to determine acceptance. The measurement shall be independent of operational variability (duty cycle, ambient conditions, operator behavior) within the scope of the worksite safety plan and operational policies. Confidence bounds or uncertainty estimates shall be reported alongside energy_per_ton to support informed decision-making. DP3 logically satisfies this revised FR by decoupling energy acquisition, tonnage acquisition, and aggregation, enabling independent validation and flexible reporting without imposing real-time compute or baseline calibration overhead.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized energy metering, tonnage integration, timestamp synchronization, aggregation logic, and efficiency metric computation with confidence bounds reporting

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receive raw energy readings from power distribution node and tonnage counts from load-bearing interface sensors on Vehicle

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmit computed energy_per_ton metric with confidence bounds and aggregation window summaries

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Synchronize timestamps across distributed measurement points; ensure clock alignment for aggregation window boundaries

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest cohesion: metering integration, tonnage counting, synchronization, aggregation, and metric computation are all analytics/reporting functions naturally owned by the solution layer. Minimal diffusion: only requires inbound telemetry and outbound reporting via standard interfaces. Timestamp sync logic can be localized within this module.

Poor fit: Vehicle owns sensors (power node, load interface) but not the aggregation, synchronization, or metric computation logic. Placing analytics here couples vehicle firmware to complex statistical logic and creates tight dependency on external clock sync. High diffusion: computation would leak into vehicle control layer.

Very poor fit: Teleoperation is a command/control interface, not a data analytics layer. Placing metering and aggregation here violates separation of concerns. High diffusion: would require teleoperation to manage sensor fusion, statistics, and reporting—unrelated to remote operation.

Poor fit: Safety Services owns alert logic and safety constraints, not energy efficiency analytics. While energy_per_ton could inform safety thresholds, the metering, aggregation, and metric computation are not safety-critical functions. Placing them here couples safety logic to efficiency reporting and creates unnecessary diffusion.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the natural owner of energy efficiency analytics. It receives telemetry streams (energy, tonnage) from Vehicle sensors, synchronizes timestamps via Network Link, aggregates over evaluation windows, and reports energy_per_ton with confidence bounds back to stakeholders via Telemetry. This keeps analytics logic centralized, avoids duplicating computation across vehicle firmware or teleoperation interfaces, and maintains clean separation between sensor acquisition (Vehicle), remote control (Teleoperation), safety enforcement (Safety Services), and efficiency reporting (NGHE Solution).
containment_risks: Clock synchronization across Vehicle and NGHE Solution may introduce latency or skew if Network Link does not guarantee tight time alignment; recommend explicit timestamp validation and windowing tolerance in aggregation logic.

If Vehicle sensors (power node, tonnage interface) are not reliably instrumented or calibrated, NGHE Solution will receive noisy or incomplete data, degrading confidence bounds; recommend sensor validation contract at Telemetry interface.

Confidence bounds computation requires statistical model (e.g., Bayesian, frequentist); if model assumptions are not documented or validated against real sensor behavior, reported bounds may be misleading; recommend explicit model specification and validation protocol.

If evaluation window duration is not synchronized with Vehicle operational cycles, aggregation may span incomplete or partial load events; recommend window definition aligned with task boundaries via Tasking interface.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: DP3 is well-scoped and logically sound. No revision needed. However, strengthen the DP specification to explicitly call out: (1) timestamp synchronization tolerance and validation rules, (2) sensor data quality assumptions and fallback behavior for missing/invalid readings, (3) statistical model for confidence bounds (e.g., normal approximation, bootstrap, Bayesian credible intervals), and (4) evaluation window definition and alignment with operational task cycles. These clarifications will reduce implementation ambiguity and improve containment verification during detailed design.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 directly and completely addresses FR requirements: (1) energy metering at power distribution node satisfies energy measurement, (2) tonnage counting at load-bearing interface satisfies work output quantification, (3) timestamp synchronization and aggregation over evaluation window satisfy temporal alignment, (4) energy_per_ton computation with confidence bounds directly maps to the acceptance criterion 'energy_per_ton ≤ E_target'. Logic is sound and traceable. No gaps detected.

diffusion_risks: Clock synchronization tolerance not explicitly specified in DP3; if Network Link latency or skew exceeds aggregation window granularity, timestamp validation may fail silently. Recommend explicit tolerance bounds (e.g., ±100ms) in DP specification.

Sensor data quality contract missing: DP3 assumes reliable energy and tonnage readings but does not specify fallback behavior for missing, invalid, or out-of-range sensor values. Risk: NGHE Solution may compute confidence bounds on incomplete data. Recommend sensor validation rules at Telemetry inbound interface.

Statistical model for confidence bounds not specified in DP3 (normal approximation vs. bootstrap vs. Bayesian). Different models yield different bounds; ambiguity creates implementation variance and verification difficulty. Recommend explicit model selection in DP.

Evaluation window definition not tied to operational task cycles in DP3. Risk: aggregation may span partial load events or incomplete work cycles, violating acceptance criterion intent. Recommend window alignment rule via Tasking interface or explicit task boundary detection logic.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution module achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. Ownership is appropriate: metering integration, tonnage fusion, synchronization, aggregation, and metric computation are cohesive analytics functions naturally localized to a dedicated efficiency reporting layer. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are correctly excluded (containment scores 0.35, 0.2, 0.25 respectively). Interfaces are clean: inbound Telemetry (energy + tonnage streams), outbound Telemetry (energy_per_ton + bounds), and Network Link (timestamp sync). No evidence of scattered responsibilities or cross-module duplication. IM boundary is well-defined and defensible.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Acceptance criterion 'energy_per_ton ≤ E_target over agreed evaluation period' is directly verifiable against NGHE Solution outputs. Metric computation is deterministic given sensor inputs and window definition. Confidence bounds enable statistical validation. Verification requires: (1) sensor calibration certificates, (2) evaluation window definition document, (3) statistical model specification, (4) test data with known energy/tonnage ratios. All are achievable with recommended DP clarifications.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR allocation to IM is clear and unambiguous. NGHE Solution owns the complete energy efficiency analytics pipeline: data ingestion (Telemetry in), processing (synchronization, aggregation, computation), and reporting (Telemetry out). Vehicle owns sensor instrumentation only (power node, load interface); Teleoperation and Safety Services have no direct role in efficiency measurement. Ownership boundaries are non-overlapping and well-justified by containment analysis. No allocation conflicts detected.

recommended_revisions: Strengthen DP3 specification to include explicit timestamp synchronization tolerance (e.g., ±100ms) and validation rules for clock skew detection and recovery.

Add sensor data quality contract to DP3: specify acceptable ranges for energy and tonnage readings, define fallback behavior for missing/invalid data (e.g., interpolation, window rejection, confidence bound widening), and require sensor validation at Telemetry inbound interface.

Explicitly specify the statistical model for confidence bounds in DP3 (e.g., normal approximation with sample variance, bootstrap percentile intervals, or Bayesian credible intervals). Include model assumptions and validation protocol against real sensor noise characteristics.

Define evaluation window alignment rule in DP3: specify whether windows are fixed-duration (e.g., 1 hour) or task-aligned (e.g., per load cycle). If task-aligned, add logic to detect task boundaries via Tasking interface or sensor state transitions.

Add acceptance test criteria to DP3: specify how confidence bounds will be validated (e.g., coverage rate ≥95% on historical data), and define minimum sample size or aggregation duration required for bounds to be reported as valid.

Document the relationship between evaluation window duration and E_target definition: confirm that E_target is defined over the same window type and duration as DP3 aggregation, to ensure acceptance criterion is correctly applied.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass logically and architecturally, confirming that the functional requirement is sound and the NGHE Solution module is a defensible implementation boundary. However, the DP-IM gate identifies six critical diffusion risks and specification gaps that, if left unresolved, create verification ambiguity and operational failure modes. Given high safety criticality, these gaps must be closed at the current level before decomposition proceeds. The risks are not architectural (IM boundary is correct) but rather specification clarity issues in DP3 that directly impact acceptance criterion validation and sensor data integrity. Revising DP3 to address timestamp tolerance, sensor quality contracts, statistical model selection, window alignment, acceptance test criteria, and E_target alignment will eliminate diffusion and enable confident downstream decomposition.

revise.why: DP3 specification is logically sound but incomplete. Six recommended revisions address critical gaps: (1) explicit timestamp synchronization tolerance (±100ms) and clock skew validation to prevent silent aggregation failures; (2) sensor data quality contract with fallback rules for missing/invalid readings to ensure confidence bounds are computed on valid data; (3) explicit statistical model selection (normal approximation, bootstrap, or Bayesian) to eliminate implementation variance and enable verification; (4) evaluation window alignment rule (fixed-duration vs. task-aligned) to ensure aggregation respects operational task cycles and acceptance criterion intent; (5) acceptance test criteria specifying confidence bound validation (e.g., ≥95% coverage) and minimum sample size to ensure metric reliability; (6) documented relationship between evaluation window duration and E_target definition to confirm acceptance criterion is correctly applied. These revisions strengthen DP3 without changing the core design logic or IM boundary. They are essential for verification feasibility and operational safety given high criticality.

assumptions: Timestamp synchronization tolerance of ±100ms is operationally acceptable for energy/tonnage aggregation granularity (to be confirmed with Telemetry and Network Link owners).
Sensor data quality contract can be defined and enforced at Telemetry inbound interface without requiring Vehicle or sensor subsystem redesign.

Statistical model for confidence bounds will be selected based on sensor noise characteristics and historical data analysis (normal approximation assumed as baseline, alternatives evaluated during DP refinement).

Evaluation window definition is task-aligned (per load cycle) rather than fixed-duration, enabling acceptance criterion to reflect actual work output efficiency (to be confirmed with Tasking interface owner).
E_target is defined over the same window type and duration as DP3 aggregation, ensuring acceptance criterion is correctly applied (to be confirmed with requirements owner).
Acceptance test criteria (e.g., ≥95% confidence bound coverage) are achievable with available historical sensor data and do not require extended field trials (to be validated during DP refinement).
open_risks: Clock synchronization tolerance not yet specified: if Network Link latency or skew exceeds ±100ms, timestamp validation may fail silently, causing energy/tonnage misalignment and incorrect efficiency metric. Mitigation: add explicit tolerance bounds and clock skew detection logic to DP3.

Sensor data quality contract missing: DP3 assumes reliable energy and tonnage readings but does not specify fallback behavior for missing, invalid, or out-of-range values. Risk: confidence bounds may be computed on incomplete data, yielding invalid acceptance criterion validation. Mitigation: define sensor validation rules and fallback strategy (interpolation, window rejection, or bound widening) at Telemetry inbound interface.

Statistical model for confidence bounds not specified: different models (normal approximation, bootstrap, Bayesian) yield different bounds. Risk: implementation variance and verification difficulty. Mitigation: explicitly select model in DP3 and validate against real sensor noise characteristics.

Evaluation window alignment not defined: if windows span partial load events or incomplete work cycles, aggregation may violate acceptance criterion intent. Risk: efficiency metric may not reflect actual operational performance. Mitigation: define window alignment rule (task-aligned vs. fixed-duration) and add task boundary detection logic if needed.

Acceptance test criteria not specified: confidence bounds may be reported without validation of coverage rate or minimum sample size. Risk: bounds may be statistically unreliable. Mitigation: add acceptance test criteria (e.g., ≥95% coverage, minimum aggregation duration) to DP3.

E_target definition not tied to evaluation window: if E_target is defined over a different window type or duration than DP3 aggregation, acceptance criterion will be incorrectly applied. Risk: efficiency metric may pass/fail based on misaligned comparison. Mitigation: document relationship between window duration and E_target definition.
evidence_needed: Timestamp synchronization tolerance specification document: define acceptable clock skew (e.g., ±100ms), clock skew detection and recovery logic, and validation protocol for Network Link synchronization.

Sensor data quality contract: specify acceptable ranges for energy and tonnage readings, define fallback behavior for missing/invalid data (interpolation, window rejection, confidence bound widening), and require sensor validation at Telemetry inbound interface.

Statistical model specification: select confidence bound model (normal approximation, bootstrap percentile intervals, or Bayesian credible intervals), document model assumptions, and provide validation protocol against real sensor noise characteristics.

Evaluation window alignment rule: specify whether windows are fixed-duration (e.g., 1 hour) or task-aligned (e.g., per load cycle). If task-aligned, provide logic to detect task boundaries via Tasking interface or sensor state transitions.

Acceptance test criteria: specify how confidence bounds will be validated (e.g., coverage rate ≥95% on historical data), define minimum sample size or aggregation duration required for bounds to be reported as valid, and provide test data with known energy/tonnage ratios.

E_target definition document: confirm that E_target is defined over the same window type and duration as DP3 aggregation, and document the relationship to ensure acceptance criterion is correctly applied.
Historical sensor data: provide representative energy and tonnage readings from operational vehicles to validate statistical model assumptions and acceptance test criteria.

Telemetry interface specification: confirm that Telemetry inbound interface can enforce sensor validation rules and that Telemetry outbound interface can report confidence bounds with metadata (model type, sample size, coverage rate).
